name: Valkyries
content:
# Valkyries

Odin's warrior maidens who select fallen heroes from battlefields to bring to Valhalla. Divine shield-maidens who serve the Einherjar mead and decide the fates of warriors in combat.

## Abilities and Powers

- Brynhildr: The most famous Valkyrie, punished by Odin for disobeying him, placed in an enchanted sleep surrounded by fire until awakened by Sigurd
- Göndul: A war Valkyrie who decides battles
- Skögul: Another battle Valkyrie, companion of Göndul
- Hildr: "Battle" - leads the eternal battle of Hjadningavig
- Geirahöd: "Spear of Battle"

## Archetypal Significance

Valkyries guide the worthy dead to their afterlife destination - divine beings who choose fate in battle.

keyMyths.title: The Valkyries Choosing the Slain
keyMyths.description: Valkyries are most famously known for their role in selecting the bravest warriors who have fallen in battle. They ride across the battlefields, often described as armored figures on horseback, and choose those worthy to be taken to Valhalla, Odin's hall.

keyMyths.title: Serving Mead in Valhalla
keyMyths.description: In Valhalla, the Valkyries serve the chosen warriors, the Einherjar, with mead and food, catering to their needs as they prepare for Ragnarok. This role highlights their nurturing and supportive aspect beyond their warrior function.

keyMyths.title: Valkyries and Fate-Weaving
keyMyths.description: Some accounts suggest Valkyries are not solely limited to battlefields but also involved in weaving the threads of fate. They influence the outcome of battles and the lifespan of individuals, connecting them to broader cosmic forces.
keyMyths.source: Njal's Saga, Darraðarljóð (part of Njal's Saga)

keyMyths.title: The Valkyrie Brunhild's Disobedience and Punishment
keyMyths.description: Brunhild, a prominent Valkyrie, disobeys Odin's orders in battle, leading to her punishment of being stripped of her Valkyrie status and placed in a ring of fire, to be awakened only by a hero worthy of her. This myth explores themes of obedience, free will, and the consequences of challenging divine authority.

sources.title: The Valkyries: The Women of Norse Myth
sources.author: Britt-Mari Näsström

sources.title: Die Walküren: Ihre Historische und Mythologische Bedeutung
sources.author: Otto Höfler

extendedContent.title: Roles and Responsibilities
extendedContent.content: The primary role of the Valkyries was to select the most valiant warriors from the slain on the battlefield and bring them to Valhalla, Odin's hall for fallen heroes. These chosen warriors, known as the Einherjar, would train and prepare for Ragnarok, the prophesied end of the world. The Valkyries were not merely transporters of souls; they also served the Einherjar in Valhalla, offering them mead and attending to their needs. Furthermore, the Valkyries possessed the power to influence the outcome of battles, choosing who would live and who would die. This aspect of their role connects them to the concept of fate and destiny, highlighting their importance in the cosmic order of Norse mythology. Some Valkyries are named, suggesting individual personalities and roles beyond the general duties assigned to them. These individual Valkyries are often associated with specific skills or attributes, further enriching the complexity of their overall depiction.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Associations
extendedContent.content: The origins of the Valkyries are debated, with some scholars suggesting they evolved from earlier Germanic concepts of female deities associated with war and death. Their connection to Odin is undeniable, as they are often referred to as his daughters or handmaidens. However, some sources also link them to other gods and goddesses, suggesting a more complex and multifaceted origin. The Valkyries are also associated with ravens, wolves, and horses, all symbols of war and death in Norse mythology. These associations further reinforce their role as intermediaries between the human and divine realms, particularly in matters of conflict and mortality. The concept of the Valkyrie has also been linked to shamanistic practices and ecstatic religious experiences, suggesting a deeper connection to the spiritual beliefs of the Norse people. Their ability to traverse between worlds and influence the fates of warriors may reflect the shaman's role as a mediator between the living and the dead.

extendedContent.title: Notable Valkyries
extendedContent.content: Several Valkyries are named in Norse literature, each with their own unique characteristics and stories. Brynhildr is perhaps the most famous, appearing in both the Poetic Edda and the Völsunga saga. Her tale is one of love, betrayal, and tragic fate, as she is punished by Odin for disobeying his orders in battle. Sigrdrífa, another prominent Valkyrie, is featured in the Sigrdrífumál, where she imparts wisdom and magical knowledge to the hero Sigurd. Other notable Valkyries include Gunnr, Hildr, and Göndul, each mentioned in various poems and sagas. These individual Valkyries demonstrate the diversity and complexity of their role in Norse mythology, highlighting their agency and influence beyond their general duties. Their stories often explore themes of love, war, fate, and the consequences of defying the gods, adding depth and nuance to the overall portrayal of the Valkyries.

appearance: Valkyries are typically depicted as fair and powerful warrior women, often clad in shining armor and wielding spears or swords. They are frequently described as having long, flowing hair, sometimes blonde or red, and piercing blue or grey eyes that reflect their fierce determination. Some accounts portray them as winged beings, soaring across the battlefields to collect the souls of the slain.
shortDescription: Odin's shield-maidens, the Valkyries choose worthy fallen warriors and escort them to Valhalla, also serving mead and influencing battles.
